text: Oh, dear. Another NRC spin-off. Feels like watching my 3D printer run out of filament mid-job. Photonics folks should learn cloud skills instead.

text:
"15 minutes of exposure to this can reduce blood glucose elevation by 27.7%."
— Journal of Biophotonics, 2024

"It activates the enzyme inside your cells that produces ATP, the energy currency of every living cell."
— Frontiers in Photonics, 2024

"It improves muscle recovery, accelerates wound healing, and supports tissue repair across dermatology, neurological function, and metabolic health."
— News Medical / Photobiomodulation review, 2026

All refer to red light.

The same wavelengths your great-grandparents got at sunrise and sunset every day.

The same wavelengths almost completely missing from modern indoor life.

Combined value: ~8 billion SEK. https://t.co/k9EMCinhs5 It is framed as these investors are driving this on speculation. Yes maybe, that is of course possible. Or is there another angle? My take, SEK8B..what? Hence my reaction would probably be the opposite, this could be worth investigating: What thesis are these allocators actually trading? Is it pure speculation with SEK 8B in play? Who sits on these SEK8B? EFN however have asked "Richard Schatz, senior researcher in photonics at RISE Research Institutes of Sweden and former consultant for Sivers", for his opinion about Sivers. So what did Mr. Richard Schatz say: "There are several companies that manufacture these kinds of lasers, and Sivers is far from alone. I don't understand why the company has become so hyped. There are many players, not just in Europe and the US, but also in China," says Schatz. Honestly I have been COO and CEO at Sivers for almost 10 years 2015-2024 (2015 we had 18 headcount I known everyone in the Company until 2024) and also ask Board members that was in the board many years before me, both for Sivers and former CST Global (Sivers Photonics today) and none of them can recollect Mr. Schatz. I also sent an email, asking the reporter that interviewed Mr S and asked when he claims to have been a consultant at Sivers. Without reply. This doesn't really matter, it is just shows where the digging stops. Sivers Semiconductors is one of a handful of merchant InP laser suppliers globally. They have disclosed partnerships with POET Technologies, Jabil (1.6T LRO), O-Net, Ayar Labs and operate in the External Light Source category that scale-up CPO architectures depend on. Either are all these US investors dramatically wrong about the photonics layer of AI infrastructure, or the institutional allocators routing through US brokerages have identified what most Swedish investors haven't yet. Maybe this is worth investigating which? To be 100% clear no one knows (not even me), who has the right hypothesis, that we might know in 2028-2030 time frame. To help anyone intressted, I made this free and open substack to explain how the money flows. As they said in Washington follow the money... text:
Lumentum Holdings (LITE) — Optical networking/photonics for AI interconnects.
1. The easiest 5-10x moves in memory/storage (like early SNDK) are largely behind us. We’re now in the “higher risk, still big reward” phase.

2. Valuations in AI infrastructure are stretched. A slowdown in capex or supply catching up could cause 50%+ drawdowns (memory is cyclical as hell).

3. This is not financial advice. Past performance (even Aschenbrenner’s insane returns) is no guarantee. Size appropriately and do your own research.

text:
A great feature by MIT on Piris Lab, a Tekedia Capital portfolio company developing photonics systems to power the next-generation infrastructure of AI.

Largely, Piris Labs is building a full-stack AI inference platform designed to eliminate one of the most critical constraints in modern computing: the data movement bottleneck. By combining proprietary photonic hardware with a vertically integrated software stack, the company addresses the “memory wall” that limits the efficiency of today’s GPU-based systems.

Read here  https://t.co/0yykNVI8eL

Their approach delivers comparable performance to traditional compute clusters, at a significantly lower cost, by improving effective FLOP utilization and reducing latency. In doing so, Piris Labs is helping make the unit economics of trillion-parameter AI models truly sustainable.

Tekedia Capital >> we fund innovations

The 800G to 1.6T transition is mid-ramp now, accelerating into 2026 alongside Rubin.

Test and validation aren't optional. Every new module needs production qualification.

If silicon photonics is the wave, $VIAV is the toll booth most AI portfolios are missing.

$VIAV: $12B cap, 8.8x sales, P/E 40.

Pure-play photonics names like $LITE and $COHR trade richer because the market prices them as direct beneficiaries.

VIAV gets paid by every transceiver vendor regardless of which one wins the 1.6T design slot.

Silicon photonics is the chokepoint Nvidia's Rubin platform is built around. Copper is out, light is in across every AI server.

Inside that basket, $VIAV sits at the test layer. Every 1.6T transceiver shipped gets validated on their gear before production. https://t.co/ExNUXvx0mr

text:
Never ever FOMO. If you look hard enough, there are always new opportunities to be found.

Glass, photonics, CPUs, NeoClouds, power systems, memory, and everything semis that touches the sun will go up.

If the consensus now is that memory is simply safe to invest in and is the most commonly agreed-upon bottleneck, then the market will do the opposite in the short term, creating a new opportunity, most likely on macro bad news.

Money rewards those who are early and right (i.e. glass substrate) or patient and just on time (i.e. memory).

text:
$JEN $JNPKF the next SIVE with an existing solid business.

Jenoptik AG, a German technology company specialized in photonics, broke out today of its longterm (since 2018!) sideway range. The breakout followed the Earnings call today.

The core business includes Advanced Photonic Solutions, Smart Mobility Solutions, and Non-Photonic Portfolio. Recently Jenoptik transformed its business to a pure photonic player with the segments:

Nano-scale Micro-optics: Components for Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV) lithography.
Silicon Photonics Testing: Solutions like the UFO Probe to validate optical chips.
High-Power Diode Lasers: Essential for medical and advanced industrial manufacturing.

To highlight is its more than expected surge of backlog. The total backlog has grown significantly to €719.2 million (up from €591 million at the end of 2025).

Jenoptik is a major supplier of ASML, Broadcom, Marvell and many more.

You'll find more information on my website including proposed entry and exit point as well as live signals when I buy and sell the stock.

text:
Big kudos to @pennycheck & @ThematicTrader for uncovering to me what genuinely feels like one of the most compelling AI infrastructure stories in the market right now👏🫶🐧 🐧 🐧 

$PENG sits right in the middle of the AI buildout across memory, CPUs, CXL, and even photonics. The Integrated Memory business alone is now running at roughly an $800M annualized pace growing 63% YoY, while the market still barely prices in the AI Factory platform, 85K+ deployed GPUs, or the asymmetric upside from their photonic memory architecture ambitions.

The more I dig into the story, the more it feels like Penguin is quietly positioning itself as a critical infrastructure layer for the inference era.

Really happy I managed to add more on today’s dip. I have a strong feeling this could grow into a massive company over the next few years

text:
$AMD  buying $6.5M of $MRVL at ~$99 wasn’t charity; it was supply‑chain alignment.

Hyperscaler AI buildouts favor silicon photonics and custom interconnects, which $MRVL sells.
Decision: ride $MRVL on dips while AI capex stays hot; trim spikes into earnings headlines.

text:
From Physics classrooms in Nigeria 🇳🇬 to the semiconductor industry in the USA 🇺🇸… still feels surreal sometimes.

I’ll be speaking at the UNESCO International Day of Light 2026 organized by Photonics Nigeria on:

🔬 The Role of Precision Optics in Semiconductor Manufacturing

If you’re curious about semiconductors, photonics, optics, engineering careers, or the future of technology, this session is for you.

📅 May 16, 2026
⏰ 3:00 PM – 5:00 PM WAT
🌐 Online Event

Make sure to help share/retweet so it reach more STEM students! 

See you there! 🚀✨ #Photonics #Semiconductors #Optics #Engineering #Nigeria #ASML #STEM

text:
Some new entries / bids for new entries. 

I firmly believe Robotics will be the H2 bubble of 2026. Think space H2 2025 and photonics H1 2026. 

Aside from the DCA strategy on $BOT. We like $OUST $ABSI and $AMBA from below levels if we get them. 

There are others like $VPG, but missed that boat for my risk tolerance.

Other secondaries will benefit well in this wake on the material side like $USAR (still great entry) and $MP.

text:
What makes $M7U / Nynomic interesting is the hidden SOTP angle.

Most people only look at LayTec, but Nynomic also owns tec5, Avantes, Sensortherm, m-u-t, art photonics, LemnaTec and more.

It’s a basket of photonics/sensorics hidden champions trading like a boring turnaround.

$M7U / Nynomic bull case:

At ~€18–19, Nynomic is a ~€120–130M market cap photonics group guiding for €100–105M FY26 revenue.

That’s only ~1.2–1.3x sales.

For a European photonics/SOTP turnaround with semi, pharma, sensorics, spectroscopy and LayTec exposure, that is cheap.

text:
$POET ✨ 
just announced a major new step in its AI photonics expansion.

POET Technologies secured an initial $50M purchase commitment from Lumilens for next-generation optical networking solutions.

Even more notable:

The partnership could reportedly expand to over $500M in cumulative purchases over the next 5 years.

Key focus areas:
⚡ AI optical networking
⚡ 800G / 1.6T connectivity
⚡ Wafer-level photonic integration
⚡ Hyperscale AI data centers

This is exactly where the AI infrastructure market appears to be heading:
higher bandwidth + lower power + optical scaling.

Another great day for semiconductor, photonics, space and energy sectors today.  Overall, AI infrastructure continues to be the strongest theme in this market right now.  
Not everything is working — this is a selective market — but quality names tied to compute, memory, optical networking, power, cooling and space infrastructure are seeing strong money rotation and momentum.  The market keeps rewarding the right sectors and patience.

text:
the scale up shift to CPO/NPO/LPO seems more quickly than we expected.

the truth is MAC layer is ready, both NVLINK( and UALink(ETH) are ready for copper/optical PHY in protocol level: they are just waiting for the photonics industry to catch up.

CPO is fully verified on both NVDA switch and AVGO switch with TSMC.

Chinese CSP/GPU:
1. ALI: full optical LPO.
2. Huawei: full optical LPO.

US:
1. Google: full optical LPO + OCS
2. Nvidia: NVLINK mixed copper / optical ,  NVL72 scale-up(copper) ,  8 x NVL72 intra connection(CPO) = NVL576,  scale-out: optical.
full CPO in 2028 on Feyman(NVL1152 full CPO).

the investment to $LITE and $COHR  and $GLW shows NVIDIA is trying intensify the relation and production on optical, and shift to optical. just like one year ago Jensen visited Korea to secure the HBM.

the optical usage is 30X increase than traditional server. and the industry is not ready... as NVIDIA's development is much more fast than the photonics industry.

text:
Optical transceiver and silicon photonics packaging is the actual chokepoint in the AI infrastructure stack that the index trade misses.

Each AI cluster needs hundreds of thousands of high-bandwidth optical interconnects between racks, and packaging capacity is concentrated in a handful of Taiwanese names with multi-year qualified-vendor lead times.

The pricing power lives one step downstream of cell production where Foxconn-backed assemblers sit.

text:
$SIVE old news still very bullish 

Photonics AI interconnects target to $1.1 billion by 2028

Volume production expected to ramp by 2027 https://t.co/QDBFzmdrE6

The 10x Thesis: Identifying the Bottlenecks of the Next Decade $LWLG $SIVE $ALRIB $LPK $INFQ

This portfolio targets the "Picks and Shovels" of the fourth industrial revolution: AI infrastructure, Silicon Photonics, and next-gen Semiconductors. 

These companies aren't just market participants; they provide the critical hardware and software bridges that industry giants must cross to succeed.

1. $LWLG (Lightwave Logic)

The Bottleneck: Data centers are hitting a "speed and heat wall." Traditional silicon modulators are too slow and consume too much power for future AI needs.

The Edge: Their organic polymers enable data transmission at speeds exceeding 800G and 1.6T with significantly lower power. If this becomes the industry standard, LWLG replaces legacy photonics.

2. $SIVE (Sivers Semiconductors)

The Bottleneck: Massive AI clusters require seamless optical chip-to-chip communication, but laser sources are difficult to integrate.

The Edge: Their CW-WDM (Continuous Wave Wavelength Division Multiplexing) lasers are the "engine" for optical computing. 

3. $ALRIB (Riber SA)

The Bottleneck: You cannot build advanced compound semiconductors (like those for 5G or LiDAR) without atom-by-atom precision.

The Edge: Riber is the world leader in Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) machines. They are the gatekeepers; without their hardware, the manufacturers of specialized chips cannot exist.

4. $LPK (LPKF Laser &Electronics SE)

The Bottleneck: Organic chip substrates melt under the heat of high-end AI processors. The industry is pivoting to glass.

The Edge: Their LIDE (Laser Induced Deep Etching) technology is the only proven method to process glass-core substrates at scale. LPKF owns the key to the next generation of chip packaging.

5. $INFQ (Infleqtion)

The Bottleneck: Classical supercomputers cannot solve complex quantum physics or advanced encryption.

The Edge: Focusing on "Cold Atom" technology, they provide the hardware and software stack to make quantum computing commercial. They are building the infrastructure for a post-classical computing world.

6. $SPIR (Spire Global)

The Bottleneck: Real-time global data (weather, maritime, aviation) is often fragmented or unavailable in remote areas.

The Edge: Spire operates one of the largest Nano-Satellite constellations. They offer "Space-as-a-Service," providing high-margin, recurring data subscriptions for the global economy.

7. $WOLF (Wolfspeed)

The Bottleneck: Traditional silicon chips are inefficient for high-power applications like EV drivetrains and fast chargers.

The Edge: The global leader in Silicon Carbide (SiC). SiC chips increase EV range and reduce charging times. Wolfspeed owns the essential material for the global transition to electricity.

8. $OUST (Ouster)

The Bottleneck: For robots and autonomous vehicles to operate safely, they need to "see" in 3D with high resolutio, cameras alone aren't enough.

The Edge: By moving from analog to Digital LiDAR, Ouster has simplified the hardware into a single chip.
This makes their sensors cheaper, more reliable, and easier to integrate into "Physical AI."

9. $SVCO (Silvaco) 

The Bottleneck: Designing next-gen chips (Power, Photonics, Atomic-scale) requires specialized simulation software that standard tools can't provide.

The Edge: Silvaco provides TCAD (Technology Computer-Aided Design) software. They are a "bottleneck within a bottleneck", engineers at companies like $TSM or $NXP use it to design chips that drive the portfolio. 

10. $MRAM (Everspin Technologies)

The Bottleneck: Standard RAM is "volatile", it loses all data during a power failure, which is a disaster for industrial AI and data centers.

The Edge: They are the only commercial provider of MRAM (Magnetoresistive RAM). It is as fast as RAM but keeps data without power. It is the "forever memory" for critical AI infrastructure.

The 10x Goal: While these companies have smaller market caps, they hold the IP and market position required by giants like NVIDIA, Tesla, or Amazon. If even one of these "bottleneck" technologies becomes the global standard, the upside is exponential.

What's your favorite stock?

Not financial advice.

text:
And now Photonics Layer 1;

Layer 1 — Lasers and Light Sources
What this layer means: This is the most critical bottleneck in the entire silicon photonics stack. Silicon is an excellent medium for guiding photons, but it cannot generate them. Every silicon photonics system needs an external light source — almost always an InP-based laser. EML (Electro-Absorption Modulated Laser) and CW (Continuous Wave) lasers, high-speed photodiodes, Semiconductor Optical Amplifiers (SOA), and laser driver circuits all live in this layer. The supply crunch is real: NVIDIA has reportedly pre-allocated capacity at the world’s top EML suppliers through 2027, creating an industry-wide shortage that is reshaping the entire optical module market.

$LITE — Lumentum (also active in Layers 2, 5, 6) Currently the only supplier capable of shipping 200G-per-lane EMLs in volume production — the critical component for next-generation 1.6T pluggable transceivers. This single fact makes Lumentum arguably the most irreplaceable company in the near-term AI photonics supply chain. NVIDIA has locked in Lumentum’s EML capacity through a reported $2 billion multi-year commercial agreement. Lumentum also operates its own laser and PIC fabrication facilities (Layer 2), manufactures finished transceivers (Layer 5), and sells Optical Circuit Switch systems for data center networking (Layer 6). Active across four layers.

$COHR — Coherent (also active in Layers 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) The most vertically integrated company in the entire photonics stack. Coherent manufactures its own InP lasers (Layer 1), operates its own PIC foundry (Layer 2), assembles CPO modules (Layer 3), integrates DSP functionality (Layer 4), sells 800G and 1.6T pluggable transceivers (Layer 5), and provides coherent DCI transport systems (Layer 6). Fiscal 2025 revenue reached $5.8 billion, up 23% year-over-year. No other public company spans six layers of this chain.

$MTSI — MACOM Technology Solutions (also active in Layer 4) InP laser diode and high-speed photodiode manufacturer. MACOM’s proprietary Etched Facet Technology (EFT) enables wafer-level testing — a significant cost advantage over traditional InP production, which requires die-level cutting and testing. Also manufactures Trans-Impedance Amplifiers (TIA) and driver ICs, making MACOM active in both Layer 1 (lasers and photodiodes) and Layer 4 (signal processing components).

$AAOI — Applied Optoelectronics (also active in Layer 5) A vertically integrated laser and transceiver manufacturer based in the United States. AAOI designs its own lasers, builds them in its own fab, and ships them as finished transceiver products. Domestic U.S. manufacturing has become a strategic differentiator as supply chain sovereignty rises on the agenda of hyperscalers and government customers alike. Active in Layer 1 (laser manufacturing) and Layer 5 (transceiver sales).

$AVGO — Broadcom (also active in Layers 4 and 5) Present in Layer 1 as an EML supplier, but the center of gravity for Broadcom is in SiPh ASIC design (Layer 4) and silicon photonics transceivers (Layer 5). Broadcom’s approach integrates its own photonic chip design with laser components, producing a more unified product than competitors who source lasers externally. Active across three layers.

$AEVA — Aeva Technologies (new addition) Primary business is FMCW LiDAR, but Aeva has been moving its silicon photonics platform into data center applications. In January 2026, the company announced a high-power Semiconductor Optical Amplifier (SOA) delivering industry-leading performance for AI data center and Co-Packaged Optics architectures. The SOA addresses signal degradation in long-distance optical paths — a critical function in CPO systems. Aeva’s CoreVision module also integrates all LiDAR components onto a single SiPh chip, and this accumulated photonics expertise is being transferred toward data center use cases. Data center revenue exposure is still early-stage, but the SOA announcement is the entry point that puts AEVA in Layer 1.

$SIVE — Sivers Semiconductors Silicon-based laser and optoelectronic component developer. Working on laser integration using the Si/Ge platform rather than traditional InP — a potential cost advantage, but a technology that is still maturing toward production volumes.

$STM — STMicroelectronics (also active in Layer 4) Manufactures edge emitter lasers and optoelectronic components as part of a broad product portfolio. Also active in the mixed-signal and analog-to-digital converter segment, giving STM a presence in Layer 4 DSP support circuits as well.

$LASR — Laser component supplier (former II-VI ticker) Laser component and sub-assembly manufacturer. Now integrated under the Coherent umbrella following the merger, but still referenced separately in some industry analyses.

End of Layer 1.

Layer 1 — Lasers and Light Sources
What this layer means: This is the most critical bottleneck in the entire silicon photonics stack. Silicon is an excellent medium for guiding photons, but it cannot generate them. Every silicon photonics system needs an external light source — almost always an InP-based laser. EML (Electro-Absorption Modulated Laser) and CW (Continuous Wave) lasers, high-speed photodiodes, Semiconductor Optical Amplifiers (SOA), and laser driver circuits all live in this layer. The supply crunch is real: NVIDIA has reportedly pre-allocated capacity at the world’s top EML suppliers through 2027, creating an industry-wide shortage that is reshaping the entire optical module market.

$LITE — Lumentum (also active in Layers 2, 5, 6) Currently the only supplier capable of shipping 200G-per-lane EMLs in volume production — the critical component for next-generation 1.6T pluggable transceivers. This single fact makes Lumentum arguably the most irreplaceable company in the near-term AI photonics supply chain. NVIDIA has locked in Lumentum’s EML capacity through a reported $2 billion multi-year commercial agreement. Lumentum also operates its own laser and PIC fabrication facilities (Layer 2), manufactures finished transceivers (Layer 5), and sells Optical Circuit Switch systems for data center networking (Layer 6). Active across four layers.

$COHR — Coherent (also active in Layers 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) The most vertically integrated company in the entire photonics stack. Coherent manufactures its own InP lasers (Layer 1), operates its own PIC foundry (Layer 2), assembles CPO modules (Layer 3), integrates DSP functionality (Layer 4), sells 800G and 1.6T pluggable transceivers (Layer 5), and provides coherent DCI transport systems (Layer 6). Fiscal 2025 revenue reached $5.8 billion, up 23% year-over-year. No other public company spans six layers of this chain.

$MTSI — MACOM Technology Solutions (also active in Layer 4) InP laser diode and high-speed photodiode manufacturer. MACOM’s proprietary Etched Facet Technology (EFT) enables wafer-level testing — a significant cost advantage over traditional InP production, which requires die-level cutting and testing. Also manufactures Trans-Impedance Amplifiers (TIA) and driver ICs, making MACOM active in both Layer 1 (lasers and photodiodes) and Layer 4 (signal processing components).

$AAOI — Applied Optoelectronics (also active in Layer 5) A vertically integrated laser and transceiver manufacturer based in the United States. AAOI designs its own lasers, builds them in its own fab, and ships them as finished transceiver products. Domestic U.S. manufacturing has become a strategic differentiator as supply chain sovereignty rises on the agenda of hyperscalers and government customers alike. Active in Layer 1 (laser manufacturing) and Layer 5 (transceiver sales).

$AVGO — Broadcom (also active in Layers 4 and 5) Present in Layer 1 as an EML supplier, but the center of gravity for Broadcom is in SiPh ASIC design (Layer 4) and silicon photonics transceivers (Layer 5). Broadcom’s approach integrates its own photonic chip design with laser components, producing a more unified product than competitors who source lasers externally. Active across three layers.

$AEVA — Aeva Technologies (new addition) Primary business is FMCW LiDAR, but Aeva has been moving its silicon photonics platform into data center applications. In January 2026, the company announced a high-power Semiconductor Optical Amplifier (SOA) delivering industry-leading performance for AI data center and Co-Packaged Optics architectures. The SOA addresses signal degradation in long-distance optical paths — a critical function in CPO systems. Aeva’s CoreVision module also integrates all LiDAR components onto a single SiPh chip, and this accumulated photonics expertise is being transferred toward data center use cases. Data center revenue exposure is still early-stage, but the SOA announcement is the entry point that puts AEVA in Layer 1.

$SIVE — Sivers Semiconductors Silicon-based laser and optoelectronic component developer. Working on laser integration using the Si/Ge platform rather than traditional InP — a potential cost advantage, but a technology that is still maturing toward production volumes.

$STM — STMicroelectronics (also active in Layer 4) Manufactures edge emitter lasers and optoelectronic components as part of a broad product portfolio. Also active in the mixed-signal and analog-to-digital converter segment, giving STM a presence in Layer 4 DSP support circuits as well.

$LASR — Laser component supplier (former II-VI ticker) Laser component and sub-assembly manufacturer. Now integrated under the Coherent umbrella following the merger, but still referenced separately in some industry analyses.

End of Layer 1.

The Analytical Edge Strategy
May 11, 2026

A complete map of the silicon photonics supply chain — from raw indium to fiber in the ground

If you’ve been following AI infrastructure, you’ve probably heard that data centers are hitting what some call the Copper Wall — the point where electrical signals through copper can no longer keep up with the heat, latency, and bandwidth demands of training and running massive AI models. The answer is photonics: moving data as light through fiber instead of electrons through metal.

But photonics isn’t a single product. It’s a deep, layered supply chain stretching from exotic crystal growth in China to fiber trenching across American highways. This guide maps every layer of that chain, names every major public company in it, and explains exactly what each one does.

No data has been omitted. Every company from the original research is here.

Layer 0 — Raw Materials
What this layer means: This is the floor of the photonics supply chain. Before any chip can be designed, any laser built, or any fiber laid, the raw materials must exist. Indium phosphide (InP), silicon-on-insulator (SOI), gallium arsenide (GaAs), and optical-grade glass fiber are the fundamental outputs of this layer. Every laser, chip, and cable in the chain depends on what gets produced here. It is also the most geopolitically exposed layer in the entire stack: approximately 60–70% of global indium production comes from China.

$AXTI — AXT Inc. Single-crystal InP and GaAs wafer manufacturer. These substrates are the irreplaceable foundation for EML lasers and high-speed photodiodes. NVIDIA’s aggressive push toward 1.6T transceivers has turned InP into a supply bottleneck, and AXTI sits directly at that chokepoint.

$IQE III-V compound semiconductor epitaxy specialist. IQE grows thin-film layers of InP, GaAs, and GaN onto customer wafers, supplying the epitaxial substrate that laser diode and high-speed photodetector manufacturers need. Often called the invisible backbone of the optical module supply chain.

$SOI — Soitec Silicon-on-Insulator wafer manufacturer. SOI wafers are the starting material for silicon photonics foundries. TSMC’s COUPE platform, GlobalFoundries’ Fotonix process, and Tower Semiconductor’s SiPh line all rely on Soitec substrates. When a Broadcom or Marvell photonic chip ships inside a data center, there is a good chance the journey started on a Soitec wafer.

$WOLF — Wolfspeed Silicon carbide (SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN) wafer manufacturer. Not directly tied to photonic lasers, but critical for the high-power electronics and power converters inside data center infrastructure. Wolfspeed feeds the power layer that photonics depends on to function.

$GLW — Corning (also active in Layer 5) The world’s largest manufacturer of optical fiber and specialty glass. Every fiber cable running inside and between data centers starts here. Corning also produces specialty glass components used in transceiver housings and is a named partner in NVIDIA’s Spectrum-X silicon photonics ecosystem. Active in both Layer 0 (raw glass and fiber) and Layer 5 (module components).

$LWLG — Lightwave Logic Polymer-based electro-optic waveguide developer. Rather than silicon, LWLG uses organic polymer materials in the modulators that convert electrical signals to light — promising significantly higher modulation speeds and lower insertion loss. Still in the commercialization phase, making this an early-stage bet on a materials platform that could disrupt the conventional silicon approach.

$SHMN — Shin-Etsu Chemical (also active in Equipment layer) The world’s largest silicon wafer manufacturer. While SiPh foundries use SOI specifically, Shin-Etsu supplies the standard silicon wafer base that underpins the entire semiconductor ecosystem. Also present in the equipment layer as a foundry supply chain essential.

$VECO — Veeco Instruments (also active in Equipment layer) Manufacturer of MBE (Molecular Beam Epitaxy) and MOCVD (Metal-Organic Chemical Vapor Deposition) equipment. Veeco does not make wafers — it makes the machines that grow them. III-V laser wafer production is impossible without MOCVD reactors, which is where Veeco’s role sits. Active in both Layer 0 (enabling raw material production) and the Equipment layer.

End of Layer 0.
There are 6 layer more.

So you can read my full report in my substack as free;
https://t.co/CZIKj5KHVp

My personal insights, not financial advice (NFA). Full disclosure in my pinned tweet.

text:
OCP is excited to announce our upcoming panel at Fiber Connect 2026's AI and Emerging Technology Infrastructure Summit on Wednesday, May 20!

Panel Title: Interconnect Insights on the Photonics Enabling AI Factory Scale and the Multi-Data Center Future

Join our expert panel for this discussion:
-Moderator: James Kelly, VP Market Intelligence & Innovation, OCP
-Sherwin Cabatic, President and CEO, NTT Devices America
-Jonmichael Hands, CEO, FarmGPU
-Stephen Manley, Sr Principal Network Engineer, Oracle Cloud
-David Hjerpe, National Account Manager, Databank

The race to exascale AI is fundamentally shifting how AI factory building blocks are designed and operated. This OCP-led panel will discuss how networking and photonics are becoming critical enablers for data movement, focusing on three key areas: scale-up within an AI pod, scale-out across the AI factory floor, and scale-across between AI data center campuses and metros. One practical reality we'll be discussing is that power density isn't the only headline-grabber: for data centers, fiber count and fiber pathway capacity per rack are so demanding that the cable plant is now essentially the new floor-loading, catching many colo designs flat-footed.

We look forward to seeing you at Fiber Connect!

Learn more and register here: https://t.co/J8WVlRM8fp

#FiberConnect2026 #AI #Photonics #DataCenter #OCP #OpenComputeProject #networking

text:
This market has been full of squeezes 🍋📈

AI infrastructure.
Photonics.
Data centers.
High beta growth.
Energy.
Small caps, mid caps, mega caps, even some POS caps..
Momentum everywhere.

And yes, there’s a lot of money to be made trading these moves.

But eventually you realize the real game isn’t just catching squeezes.

It’s finding the names you actually want to keep holding while the crowd keeps overthinking every pullback.

Names where you keep following the earnings, the flows, the sector, the cycle, and the long term opportunity.

That’s how you end up staying with winners instead of constantly rotating into the next shiny object, therefore overtrading.

For me personally, names like $HOOD $AMD $INTC $RKLB $AG $PAAS $COHR $ALB became more than trades.

You can trim, hedge, trade around a core position..

But understanding the underlying business matters.

Or if it’s early stage, at least understanding the theme and positioning through options flows.

But at some point you need more than drawing a few lines on a chart and pretending price owes you something because of your pretty drawings.

Know what you own.

And if you truly believe in the thesis, and you nailed the entry, don’t rush to sell just because volatility shows up and people telling you it’s worthless.
Those people might eventually chase your stocks higher.

text:
There are a lot of dumbasses who think that each  of 200 photonics companies will simply x5 in a week. Some will some will not. 
Using NVDA in hindsight makes no sense. I could find 20 other companies who had life changing products and got shut down in 2 years. 

Not a value investor, just that I want to know what I'm buying and what I'm expecting it to be in the future. 

I'm not trying to convince you and it's fine if you disagree.

text: Idk humans can adapt pretty fast, and I doubt we’ll ever go truly backwards, only forwards in a 2 step forward and 1-3 steps back pattern like we’ve always done as a species. Where are we headed?  I think towards infinite free energy, photonics and quantum as basis of computing 1000x more powerful and energy efficient then it is today, and also warping our way in the galaxy between bases on different planets.  All of that is easily within laws of physics, and is yet to come for humanity imho, maybe not all in our lifetimes but all seems doable.  Digital is just a stepping stone, imho.  yes it’s fricking messy, and human brains not coping very well, idk.

text:
רוצה להציג בפניכם ETF חדש לתחום חם מאוד בשנה האחרונה. וכתבתי עליו בעבר גם. והוא כמובן הפוטוניקה והליטוגרפיה. אני באופן אישי עוקבת אחרי כמה חברות בתחום..

לפני 8 ימים השיקה Corgi Funds את -
 Corgi Lithography & Semiconductor Photonics ETF $EUV 

אז הETF הנ״ל משקיע באופן אקטיבי בחברות ששולטות בטכנולוגיית האור (Photonics) ובשרשרת ייצור השבבים המתקדמים ליתוגרפיית EUV, לייזרים, רכיבים אופטיים, סיליקון פוטוניקס ועוד.. כמעט כל שבבי ה AI תלויים בטכנולוגיה הזו והETF הזה נותן את החשיפה בלי לבחור מניה אחת בודדת.

ומי אלה הCorgi האלו? אז היא חברה צעירה שהוקמה לפני שנתיים בסן פרנסיסקו, בוגרי Y Combinator. הם התחילו כפלטפורמת ביטוח AI native לחברות טק וסטארטאפים, גייסו והגיעו לשווי חברה של 1.3 מיליארד דולר. הם מביאים גישה שלהם שהיא מאוד חדשנית, טכנולוגית ואגרסיבית גם לעולם הקרנות.. הם מתמחים במוצרים תמטיים ממוקדים מאוד, אקטיביים עם עמלות תחרותיות (לרוב 0.35% בלבד).

5 האחזקות המובילות בקרן (נכון ל-14.5.2026):
$TSM  - Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSMC)
$ASML  - ASML Holding
$GLW - Corning
$LRCX  - Lam Research
$AMAT - Applied Materials

 בעיני זה יכול להתאים למי שמאמין שהביקוש לשבבי AI רק הולך וגדל ורוצה חשיפה חדה יותר לטכנולוגיה שמאפשרת את זה  ולא רק לאנבידיה או ל Mag7..
או רוצה לגוון ולא רק תעודות שבבים ״רגילות״ כמו $SMH או $SOXX למשל.

4 סיבות שהם מציינים למה לדעתם כדאי להשקיע בקרן:
שוק ציוד ייצור השבבים העולמי שובר שיאים חדשים.
$ASML  - הספק היחיד של ליתוגרפיית EUV - עם הזמנות ענקיות.
$TSM מגבירה את ההשקעות הענקיות בציוד ובמפעלים.
שוק הסיליקון פוטוניקס מתפוצץ בזכות דרישת ה AI.

הסיכונים וחובה להזכיר אותם אז מדובר על מכשיר פיננסי חדש מאוד, תנודתי, ניהול אקטיבי ואין היסטוריית ביצועים ארוכה..

אגב יש להם עוד ETF מעניינים בתחומים אחרים.. ממליצה להציץ.

לינק לדף הרשמי של הקרן: https://t.co/rfgIMNSG9p
 
מקווה שאהבתם.

*המידע המוצג אינו מהווה ייעוץ השקעות, המלצה לקנייה, מכירה או החזקה.. עשו מחקר עצמאי לפני פעולה כלשהי...

ששולטות בטכנולוגיית האור (Photonics) ובשרשרת ייצור השבבים המתקדמים ליתוגרפיית EUV, לייזרים, רכיבים אופטיים, סיליקון פוטוניקס ועוד.. כמעט כל שבבי ה AI תלויים בטכנולוגיה הזו והETF הזה נותן את החשיפה בלי לבחור מניה אחת בודדת. ומי אלה הCorgi האלו? אז היא חברה צעירה שהוקמה לפני שנתיים בסן פרנסיסקו, בוגרי Y Combinator. הם התחילו כפלטפורמת ביטוח AI native לחברות טק וסטארטאפים, גייסו והגיעו לשווי חברה של לשבבי AI רק הולך וגדל ורוצה חשיפה חדה יותר לטכנולוגיה שמאפשרת את זה ולא רק לאנבידיה או ל Mag7.. או רוצה לגוון ולא רק תעודות שבבים ״רגילות״ כמו $SMH או $SOXX למשל. 4 סיבות שהם מציינים למה לדעתם כדאי להשקיע בקרן: שוק ציוד ייצור השבבים העולמי שובר שיאים חדשים. $ASML - הספק היחיד של ליתוגרפיית EUV - עם הזמנות ענקיות. $TSM מגבירה את ההשקעות הענקיות בציוד ובמפעלים. שוק הסיליקון פוטוניקס מתפוצץ בזכות דרישת ה AI. הסיכונים וחובה להזכיר אותם אז מדובר על מכשיר פיננסי חדש מאוד, תנודתי, ניהול אקטיבי ואין היסטוריית ביצועים ארוכה.. אגב יש להם עוד ETF מעניינים בתחומים אחרים.. ממליצה להציץ. לינק לדף הרשמי text:
Who Wins the Photonics Stack: A Tier-by-Tier Financial Classification of Every Company

Knowing the players in the Silicon Photonics supply chain is one thing—knowing which ones are structurally and financially built to dominate is another.

In my latest deep dive, I’ve classified every public company across all 8 layers of the stack into three distinct financial tiers. We move beyond the "science projects" to identify the actual bedrock of the AI optical backbone.

The Classification Matrix:

Tier-1: The Bedrock. Proven revenue, dominant market share, and irreplaceable moat (e.g., $ASML, $LITE, $TSM, $FN).

Tier-2: The Scale-ups. Clear growth thesis, but still consolidating market position (e.g., $SOI, $IQE, $CAMT).

Moonshots: High-asymmetry bets. Zero/minimal revenue today, but own the tech that could disrupt everything (e.g., $AXTI, $LWLG, $HIMX).

Key Architectural Insights:

Vertical Integration: Why $COHR is currently the only benchmark spanning 6 layers.

The Foundry Shift: How $TSM’s entry is re-rating the entire Layer 2 landscape.

Supply Bottlenecks: Identifying the companies that own the "chokepoint" materials like InP.

Personally, I see this tiering as the essential roadmap for anyone serious about AI infrastructure. The "AI trade" is shifting from the chip to the fabric, and these are the tiers that matter.

Read the full financial map here: https://t.co/auOeAcLwkl 

My personal insights, not financial advice (NFA). Full disclosure in my pinned tweet.

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 497}, {"from_index": 865, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 892}]}, "text": "Who Wins the Photonics Stack: A Tier-by-Tier Financial Classification of Every Company Knowing the players in the Silicon Photonics supply chain is one thing—knowing which ones are structurally and financially built to dominate is another. In my latest deep dive, I’ve classified every public company across all 8 layers of the stack into three distinct financial tiers. We move beyond the "science projects" to identify the actual bedrock of the AI optical backbone. The Classification Matrix: Tier-1: The Bedrock. Proven revenue, dominant market share, and irreplaceable moat (e.g., $ASML, $LITE, $TSM, $FN). Tier-2: The Scale-ups. Clear growth thesis, but still consolidating market position (e.g., $SOI, $IQE, $CAMT). Moonshots: High-asymmetry bets. Zero/minimal revenue today, but own the tech that could disrupt everything (e.g., $AXTI, $LWLG, $HIMX). Key Architectural Insights: Vertical Integration: Why $COHR is currently the only benchmark spanning 6 layers. The Foundry Shift: How $TSM’s entry is re-rating the entire Layer 2 landscape. Supply Bottlenecks: Identifying the companies that own the "chokepoint" materials like InP. Personally, I see this tiering as the essential roadmap for anyone serious about AI infrastructure. The "AI trade" is shifting from the chip to the fabric, and these are the tiers that matter. Read the full financial map here: https://t.co/auOeAcLwkl My personal insights, not financial advice (NFA). text:
A friend texted me this week:

“Did you see $AAOI? What happened?”

What happened is that I bought it at $90 and he didn’t.

He’s not a bad investor. He’s a smart person. 

He looked at $AAOI when I first mentioned it and said:

“It’s a small photonics company. The market cap is tiny. Too risky.”

I said: “The market cap is tiny because the market hasn’t priced the BOTTLENECK yet.”

Data centers at hyperscale cannot move AI-speed data between chips using copper. 

The physics fail at that bandwidth. Optical transceivers are not optional, they are required by the laws of physics.

$AAOI manufactures the laser components that make it work.

> $124M in 800G transceiver orders from a single hyperscaler.

> 2026 guidance at $1B+. Rosenblatt raised to $220.

> Stock at $191 today from a $90 entry.

He said: “I should have listened.”

He had the same information I had. 

The difference was the framework.

I’m still LONG.

The photonics bottleneck has not been solved. It is still playing out.

-BP

Not financial advice. Your money, your decision.

But I think you are asking the wrong question.

I think you should be asking “what can photonics be applied to other than data centers?”

This shift from copper to light is a revolution. Data centers are just first.

text:
Starting from optics/photonics. How far can I go?

I am an optical/photonics engineer. The reason I started PhotonCap was simple. I wanted to see how the lasers, optical fibers, and transceivers I have worked with every day get priced in the stock market. When someone who knows the defect density of an InP epi wafer looks at the stock price of the company that makes it, sometimes you can see things the market is missing. That was the starting point.

The glass substrate investment map was optics. CPO transceivers, SiPh, InP lasers. Right in the center of what I do. The LEO satellite map was a step further out. The optical component inside an OISL terminal is my territory, but orbital mechanics and launch vehicle economics are not. The subsea cable map went further still. The III-V pump laser inside an EDFA repeater is my territory, but the cable-laying vessel market and national security framing are not.

To be honest, there are limits to interpreting fields outside my expertise from a perspective that is also outside my expertise. I can find the number that says 60 cable-laying vessels exist worldwide, but someone in the shipbuilding industry understands the operating economics of a single vessel better than I do. I can back-calculate that NEC’s subsea revenue is in the single-digit percentage range, but an analyst covering Japanese IT conglomerates will do a better job valuing that business unit than I can.

Sometimes subscribers reach out. “Analyze XX purely from XX perspective.” When I get these requests, it weighs on my conscience. Is it right to publish analysis, behind a paywall, on a topic where I am not certain whether my interpretation is correct or not?

So I thought about it seriously.

Here is what I can and cannot do. Explaining why a material is a bottleneck at the optical component tier, why a process is difficult, why a qualification takes years. That is something I can do. Macro, geopolitics, valuation models, shipbuilding, power infrastructure. These are areas where I have no hands-on engineering experience. In these areas, I am a researcher reading and cross-checking public sources, not a practitioner with field experience.

Until now, I have mostly interpreted things through an optics lens. Going forward, I want to widen the aperture slightly, expanding from optics into a broader engineering perspective. Not just photonics, but compound semiconductor manufacturing, packaging, system integration, reliability testing. A wider engineering layer for looking at supply chains, while being clear about which layers I have touched firsthand and which I have not.

What changes is the scope. What does not change is the principle. Primary sources first. Limits of inference stated explicitly. When I do not know, I say I do not know. That stays the same.

I am continuing to think about the topics you have been requesting. Within the range I can cover, I will write as honestly as I can.

Best Regards,
PhotonCap

The III-V pump laser inside an EDFA repeater is my territory, but the cable-laying vessel market and national security framing are not. To be honest, there are limits to interpreting fields outside my expertise from a perspective that is also outside my expertise. I can find the number that says 60 cable-laying vessels exist worldwide, but someone in the shipbuilding industry understands the operating economics of a single vessel better than I do. I can back-calculate that NEC’s subsea revenue is in the single-digit percentage range, but an analyst covering Japanese IT conglomerates will do a better job valuing that business unit than I can. Sometimes subscribers reach out. “Analyze XX purely from XX perspective.” When I get these requests, it weighs on my conscience. Is it right to publish analysis, behind a paywall, on a topic where I am not certain whether my interpretation is correct or not? So I thought about it seriously. Here is what I can and cannot do. Explaining why a material is a bottleneck at the optical component tier, why a process is difficult, why a qualification takes years. That is something I can do. Macro, geopolitics, valuation models, shipbuilding, power infrastructure. These are areas where I have no hands-on engineering experience. In these areas, I am a researcher reading and cross-checking public sources, not a practitioner with field experience. Until now, I have mostly interpreted things through an optics lens. Going forward, I want to widen the aperture slightly, expanding from optics into a broader engineering perspective. Not just photonics, but compound semiconductor manufacturing, packaging, system integration, reliability testing. A wider engineering layer for looking at supply chains, while being clear about which layers I have touched firsthand and which I have not. What changes is the scope. What does not change is the principle. Primary sources first. Limits of inference stated explicitly. When I do not know, I say I do not know. That stays the same. I am continuing to think about the topics you have been requesting. Within the range I can cover, I will write as honestly as I can. Best Regards, PhotonCap"}}}, "rest_id": "2054281728492285988", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "10228", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Marvell's primary CPO design is centered on its 6.4T 3D Silicon Photonics (SiPho) Light Engine — the industry’s first demonstrated 200 Gbps-per-lane optical engine for co-packaged optics (announced and live-demonstrated at OFC 2024). This is a fabless-designed, highly integrated optical “chiplet” that Marvell licenses or supplies to hyperscalers and custom XPU customers. $MRVL 

It serves as the foundational building block for both:
- CPO integration directly into custom AI accelerators (XPUs + HBM + optics on one substrate), and
- Linear-drive pluggable optics (LPO) or on-board optics as a bridge solution.

Core Technical Specs of the 6.4T 3D SiPho Light Engine

- Bandwidth: 6.4 Tbps aggregate (32 lanes × 200 Gbps PAM4, both electrical and optical interfaces).

- Power efficiency: < 5 pJ/bit (including external laser power) under typical conditions — roughly 30% lower per bit than previous 100G-generation engines.

- Integration level: Hundreds of components consolidated into a single compact package:
    -- Photonic IC (PIC): Silicon photonics waveguides, modulators, germanium photodetectors (Ge PDs), and fiber couplers.
    -- Electronic IC (EIC): Linear modulator drivers, transimpedance amplifiers (TIAs), equalization, and high-speed SerDes interfaces.
    -- Embedded microcontroller + firmware: For real-time calibration, monitoring, thermal stabilization, and link management.
    -- Passives, power distribution, and test circuitry.

- Laser: External continuous-wave (CW) laser source (fiber-delivered) for thermal and reliability reasons.

-Fiber attach: Supports detachable optical connectors (e.g., SENKO’s 36-channel metallic PIC connector) for field serviceability — critical when a 100T+ system may require thousands of fibers.

- Packaging: Advanced 3D heterogeneous integration (EIC face-to-face or side-by-side with PIC, often using TSVs, hybrid bonding, or interposers). The engine has a BGA-style electrical interface for direct attachment to the host substrate.

How It Integrates with Custom XPU Architectures

Marvell’s breakthrough CPO architecture (announced early 2025) lets customers build a single substrate that co-packages:
- One or more XPU compute dies
- HBM memory stacks
- Multiple 6.4T (or 1.6T) SiPho light engines
- High-speed SerDes + die-to-die interfaces

The light engines sit right next to the compute dies. Optical fibers exit the package via detachable connectors, enabling rack-scale or row-scale optical connectivity without copper cables. This delivers:
- 2× bandwidth density vs. 100G solutions
- Dramatically lower power and latency
- Easier thermal management (optics can be placed optimally)

Post-Acquisition Photonic Fabric™

Marvell acquired Celestial AI to accelerate scale-up interconnects. Their PFLink™ (Photonic Fabric Link) chiplet builds on similar CPO principles but targets even higher performance:
- First-gen: 16 Tbps per chiplet
- Uses EAM (electro-absorption) modulators on the PIC + TSMC 4/5nm EIC
- In-package optics + memory semantic FLITs + in-network processing
- Enables multi-rack optical fabrics with <150 ns XPU-to-XPU latency and ~2.5 pJ/bit full link power

This is a fabless-designed, highly integrated optical “chiplet” that Marvell licenses or supplies to hyperscalers and custom XPU customers. $MRVL It serves as the foundational building block for both: - CPO integration directly into custom AI accelerators (XPUs + HBM + optics on one substrate), and - Linear-drive pluggable optics (LPO) or on-board optics as a bridge solution. Core Technical Specs of the 6.4T 3D SiPho Light Engine - Bandwidth: 6.4 Tbps aggregate (32 lanes × 200 Gbps PAM4, both electrical and optical interfaces). - Power efficiency: < 5 pJ/bit (including external laser power) under typical conditions — roughly 30% lower per bit than previous 100G-generation engines. - Integration level: Hundreds of components consolidated into a single compact package: -- Photonic IC (PIC): Silicon photonics waveguides, modulators, germanium photodetectors (Ge PDs), and fiber couplers. -- Electronic IC (EIC): Linear modulator drivers, transimpedance amplifiers (TIAs), equalization, and high-speed SerDes interfaces. -- Embedded microcontroller + firmware: For real-time calibration, monitoring, thermal stabilization, and link management. -- Passives, power distribution, and test circuitry. - Laser: External continuous-wave (CW) laser source (fiber-delivered) for thermal and reliability reasons. -Fiber attach: Supports detachable optical connectors (e.g., SENKO’s 36-channel metallic PIC connector) for field serviceability — critical when a 100T+ system may require thousands of fibers. - Packaging: Advanced 3D heterogeneous integration (EIC face-to-face or side-by-side with PIC, often using TSVs, hybrid bonding, or interposers). The engine has a BGA-style electrical interface for direct attachment to the host substrate. How It Integrates with Custom XPU Architectures Marvell’s breakthrough CPO architecture (announced early 2025) lets customers build a single substrate that co-packages: - One or more XPU compute dies - HBM memory stacks - Multiple 6.4T (or 1.6T) SiPho light engines - High-speed SerDes + die-to-die interfaces The light engines sit right next to the compute dies. Optical fibers exit the package via detachable connectors, enabling rack-scale or row-scale optical connectivity without copper cables. This delivers: - 2× bandwidth density vs. 100G solutions - Dramatically lower power and latency - Easier thermal management (optics can be placed optimally) Post-Acquisition Photonic Fabric™ Marvell acquired Celestial AI to accelerate scale-up interconnects. Their PFLink™ (Photonic Fabric Link) chiplet builds on similar CPO principles but targets even higher performance: - First-gen: 16 Tbps per chiplet - Uses EAM (electro-absorption) modulators on the PIC + TSMC 4/5nm EIC - In-package optics + memory semantic FLITs + in-network processing - Enables multi-rack optical fabrics with <150 ns XPU-to-XPU latency and ~2.5 pJ/bit full link power"}}}, Short on-package electrical https://t.co/kbjVtF7Qkh", "id_str": "2055006412573728946", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "possibly_sensitive": false, "possibly_sensitive_editable": true, "quote_count": 1, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "174073808"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNTUwMDY0MTI0MTg1MTkwNDA=", "media": {"inline_media": []}, "richtext": {"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "How the CPO Process Works (Step-by-Step Signal Flow) Here's the typical end-to-end flow in a CPO system: 1. Electrical signals leave the host chip (e.g., switch ASIC or XPU): High-speed SerDes (serializer/deserializer) outputs electrical data. 2. Short on-package electrical interconnect to the optical engine (usually via micro-bumps, copper pillars, or redistribution layers on the substrate/interposer). This path is only mm long → minimal power loss and signal degradation. 3. Electronic IC (EIC) in the optical engine: Receives the electrical signal and drives the photonic components (e.g., modulator drivers, transimpedance amplifiers/TIAs). 4. Photonic IC (PIC) does the conversion: - Transmit (Tx): Continuous-wave laser light (usually external, via fiber) enters the PIC. The EIC drives modulators (e.g., micro-ring modulators/MRMs) on the PIC to encode the data onto the light. - Receive (Rx): Incoming light from fiber hits photodetectors on the PIC, which convert it to electrical current. The EIC amplifies and processes it. 5. Fiber coupling: Light enters/exits the PIC via precise couplers (grating couplers with micro-lenses or edge couplers) connected to a fiber array unit (FAU). Fibers then run to the chassis faceplate or other packages. 6. Back to the host chip on the receive side (short electrical path again). The optical engine is typically a 3D-stacked or side-by-side EIC + PIC die (often using hybrid bonding or 2.5D/3D-IC tech). The whole thing sits on an organic substrate or silicon interposer alongside the main ASIC, with external lasers (for reliability/thermal reasons) or sometimes integrated ones. Key enablers: - Silicon photonics (SiPh) on the PIC: Waveguides, MRMs (tiny resonant rings for efficient, compact 200 Gbps+ modulation), germanium photodetectors. - Advanced packaging: 2.5D (interposer), 3D stacking, CoWoS (Chip on Wafer on Substrate), etc. - Fiber attach: Sub-micron precision alignment (challenging but solved with micro-lenses/grating couplers)."}}}, "rest_id": "2055006412573728946", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "195", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2055012160820891651", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "109", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Cpo stock down a lot today due to this news? “TSMC's COUPE (Compact Universal Photonic Engine) is a cutting-edge silicon photonics platform designed for Co-Packaged Optics (CPO), aimed at accelerating chip-to-chip communication in AI data centers. By directly integrating optical engines with high-performance compute silicon (like GPUs or switches) on a 2.5D/3D package, COUPE dramatically reduces power consumption and boosts data bandwidth, addressing the limitations of traditional copper-based interconnects. 

Ansys
 +4
Key Function: COUPE uses SOIC-X to stack an electrical die directly on top of a photonic die, resulting in a compact "engine" that enables ultra-high-density interconnects.
Production Roadmap: TSMC plans to introduce COUPE-based products in 2025 for validation, with mass production scheduled for 2026 integrated into its CoWoS-integrated CPO packages.
Performance Impact: This technology aims to deliver a 2x reduction in power consumption and a 10x reduction in latency compared to existing solutions.
Market Impact: The technology is heavily supported by major industry players like NVIDIA and AMD for their next-generation AI platforms.
Technical Advantages: COUPE features embedded micro-lens arrays, a backside metal reflector, and a grating coupler to minimize optical insertion loss, keeping it below 1.2 dB. 

Design And Reuse
 +6
By moving from pluggable transceivers to on-package CPO using COUPE, data centers can achieve significant power savings, especially in massive AI clusters where data movement is the primary energy cost. “

text:
The Photonics Stack: A Layer-by-Layer Guide to Every Company Building the Optical Backbone of AI
The Analytical Edge Strategy
May 11, 2026

A complete map of the silicon photonics supply chain — from raw indium to fiber in the ground

If you’ve been following AI infrastructure, you’ve probably heard that data centers are hitting what some call the Copper Wall — the point where electrical signals through copper can no longer keep up with the heat, latency, and bandwidth demands of training and running massive AI models. The answer is photonics: moving data as light through fiber instead of electrons through metal.

But photonics isn’t a single product. It’s a deep, layered supply chain stretching from exotic crystal growth in China to fiber trenching across American highways. This guide maps every layer of that chain, names every major public company in it, and explains exactly what each one does.

No data has been omitted. Every company from the original research is here.

Layer 0 — Raw Materials
What this layer means: This is the floor of the photonics supply chain. Before any chip can be designed, any laser built, or any fiber laid, the raw materials must exist. Indium phosphide (InP), silicon-on-insulator (SOI), gallium arsenide (GaAs), and optical-grade glass fiber are the fundamental outputs of this layer. Every laser, chip, and cable in the chain depends on what gets produced here. It is also the most geopolitically exposed layer in the entire stack: approximately 60–70% of global indium production comes from China.

$AXTI — AXT Inc. Single-crystal InP and GaAs wafer manufacturer. These substrates are the irreplaceable foundation for EML lasers and high-speed photodiodes. NVIDIA’s aggressive push toward 1.6T transceivers has turned InP into a supply bottleneck, and AXTI sits directly at that chokepoint.

$IQE III-V compound semiconductor epitaxy specialist. IQE grows thin-film layers of InP, GaAs, and GaN onto customer wafers, supplying the epitaxial substrate that laser diode and high-speed photodetector manufacturers need. Often called the invisible backbone of the optical module supply chain.

$SOI — Soitec Silicon-on-Insulator wafer manufacturer. SOI wafers are the starting material for silicon photonics foundries. TSMC’s COUPE platform, GlobalFoundries’ Fotonix process, and Tower Semiconductor’s SiPh line all rely on Soitec substrates. When a Broadcom or Marvell photonic chip ships inside a data center, there is a good chance the journey started on a Soitec wafer.

$WOLF — Wolfspeed Silicon carbide (SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN) wafer manufacturer. Not directly tied to photonic lasers, but critical for the high-power electronics and power converters inside data center infrastructure. Wolfspeed feeds the power layer that photonics depends on to function.

$GLW — Corning (also active in Layer 5) The world’s largest manufacturer of optical fiber and specialty glass. Every fiber cable running inside and between data centers starts here. Corning also produces specialty glass components used in transceiver housings and is a named partner in NVIDIA’s Spectrum-X silicon photonics ecosystem. Active in both Layer 0 (raw glass and fiber) and Layer 5 (module components).

$LWLG — Lightwave Logic Polymer-based electro-optic waveguide developer. Rather than silicon, LWLG uses organic polymer materials in the modulators that convert electrical signals to light — promising significantly higher modulation speeds and lower insertion loss. Still in the commercialization phase, making this an early-stage bet on a materials platform that could disrupt the conventional silicon approach.

$SHMN — Shin-Etsu Chemical (also active in Equipment layer) The world’s largest silicon wafer manufacturer. While SiPh foundries use SOI specifically, Shin-Etsu supplies the standard silicon wafer base that underpins the entire semiconductor ecosystem. Also present in the equipment layer as a foundry supply chain essential.

$VECO — Veeco Instruments (also active in Equipment layer) Manufacturer of MBE (Molecular Beam Epitaxy) and MOCVD (Metal-Organic Chemical Vapor Deposition) equipment. Veeco does not make wafers — it makes the machines that grow them. III-V laser wafer production is impossible without MOCVD reactors, which is where Veeco’s role sits. Active in both Layer 0 (enabling raw material production) and the Equipment layer.

End of Layer 0.
There are 6 layer more.

So you can read my full report in my substack as free;
https://t.co/CZIKj5KHVp

My personal insights, not financial advice (NFA). Full disclosure in my pinned tweet.

text:
POET Technologies ($POET) – One of the cleanest asymmetric setups in AI infrastructure right now
The market is still treating the Marvell/Celestial AI order cancellation like it’s an existential event.
Reality: POET still has a very strong remaining pipeline in AI photonics and optical engines for data centers. The stock crashed 47% on the news, recovered most of it, and the downside is now largely priced in.
AI optical interconnect TAM is exploding — and POET is one of the few pure-play names positioned here.
Trade Setup:
•  Entry: Here (~$13–$14) or on a dip to $12.50 support
•  Stop: Below $10.50 (breaks the post-crash structure)
•  Target: $22–$25 (first major resistance → 70–90% upside)
Risk/reward is heavily skewed in our favor because the bad news is already digested, while the multi-customer AI boom is still in early innings.
This isn’t a “moonshot” — it’s a high-conviction setup where the market is mispricing the durability of the remaining business.
Who else is watching $POET?
In at current levels or waiting for $12.50? This is not financial advice do your own research.
Drop your thoughts 👇
#POET #AIOptics #Semiconductors #AIInfrastructure

["Bold"], "to_index": 3769}, {"from_index": 3821, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 3846}]}, "text": "When you zoom in on $SIVE photonics platform, one truth stands out: this isn’t just another laser supplier riding the AI hype, it’s the enabling backbone quietly unlocking scalable, energy-efficient data movement at the heart of hyperscale AI clusters. And the deepened partnership with Ayar Labs on co-packaged optics (CPO) is one of the strongest signals yet that this AI infrastructure opportunity is moving from early demos into manufacturing scale. Back in December 2024, $SIVE and Ayar Labs expanded their collaboration into a full strategic framework. What began with successful joint demonstrations of $SIVE high-precision multi-wavelength DFB laser arrays integrated into Ayar Labs SuperNova light source, including demos with $INTC has now progressed into product qualification, manufacturing readiness, and preparations for volume production. This is the technology powering Ayar Labs TeraPHY optical I/O chiplet, the industry’s first UCIe-compliant solution delivering up to 8 Tbps bi-directional bandwidth (scaling toward 16 Tbps with SuperNova) at a fraction of the power and latency of traditional pluggable optics. The mission is simple: eliminate the data-movement wall constraining next-generation AI clusters. What makes this even more compelling is the ecosystem momentum around Ayar. Alchip Technologies, a major player in advanced ASIC packaging and design has chosen Ayar Labs for upcoming CPO architectures. Importantly, $SIVE stands as the sole supplier to Ayar in this context. Alchip is already heavily engaged with AWS on the Trainium series, particularly Trainium4, with expectations that CPO could play a larger role in future generations such as Trainium5 around the 1.4nm node in 2029. This creates a pathway not just for AWS but potentially other hyperscalers pursuing custom silicon. The investor lineup behind Ayar Labs speaks volumes. $NVDA, $AMD, and $INTC Capital are all involved. In the recent $500M Series E, valuing Ayar at ~$3.75B, Alchip joined alongside MediaTek, ARK Invest, and Sequoia Global Equities. These are deliberate ecosystem bets on optical interconnects becoming foundational for future AI scale with strong ties to the $TSM advanced packaging ecosystem. Leadership continuity strengthens the picture. Mark Wade now serves as CEO, while former CEO Charlie Wuischpard moved to $NVDA as VP of North America & Latin America sales, adding credibility within the AI chip ecosystem. What truly distinguishes this collaboration is the depth. Ayar Labs has participated in multiple $SIVE CMDs and joint engagements. That repeated involvement points to a genuine strategic partnership rather than a transactional supplier arrangement. Additional momentum comes from collaborations with Wiwynn for rack-scale AI systems and broader industry discussions involving players such as $ALAB. The broader market remains focused on $SIVE nearer-term drivers in Lidar, mmWave, and SATCOM. Yet the Ayar Labs CPO opportunity represents the high-volume, high-margin AI data center ramp many investors have been anticipating. With early systems in the field, qualification advancing, and hyperscaler interest building, $SIVE role as the critical laser provider looks increasingly entrenched in 2027. This is why the $453 million opportunity pipeline feels conservative. One unified photonics platform addressing the power wall in AI clusters while supporting multiple revenue streams across connectivity and infrastructure, that multi-domain strength is what sets $SIVE apart. The CPO/AI infrastructure piece is advancing steadily. Same core laser technology. Same experienced team. Different verticals with converging timelines. That’s not a single-product story. That’s a platform story. And the more you connect the dots, $NVDA and $AMD backing, Alchip’s strategic moves, sole supplier positioning, and ties into the wider $TSM/MediaTek ecosystem, the more $SIVE emerges as one of the most underappreciated opportunities in the AI+photonics landscape."}}}, "rest_id": "2053096789608222766", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "183235", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2053143568751996940", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1958", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
🚨 Big step forward for Lightwave Logic $LWLG 🚨

Lightwave Logic just announced the availability of Version 1.1 of its Polymer Photonics PDK — a major upgrade that advances backend-of-line (BEOL) process integration and paves the way for high-volume foundry transfer.
This isn’t just another incremental update.

✅ Enhanced design rules, simulation models & component libraries

✅ Wafer-level poling and testing for scalability

✅ Generation 4 encapsulation for better long-term reliability

✅ Ready for transfer to production foundries (majority of work targeted for H2 2026)

Why this matters:
Polymer electro-optic technology has the potential to deliver ultra-high-speed, ultra-low-power optical interconnects — exactly what data centers and AI infrastructure desperately need as bandwidth demands explode. Moving from lab/materials innovation to manufacturable silicon photonics platforms is a critical commercialization milestone.

This brings LWLG closer to real-world adoption in next-gen photonic integrated circuits (PICs).

text:
Their official product page literally says:
"Sivers Photonics' DFB laser chips deliver compact, stable, and efficient light sources for consumer sensing applications — from biometric and health monitoring..."

Target market: "wearables, AR/VR systems, health monitors"

Their old partner Rockley Photonics (LON: RKLY) — which had Apple as biggest customer with $70M+ NRE — went bankrupt in Jan 2023.

Apple ended that relationship and pulled the project in-house under the secretive Exploratory Design Group (XDG).

text:
It's a hard physics rule. Silicon's indirect bandgap means every silicon photonics device needs an EXTERNAL III-V laser source (usually InP — indium phosphide).

Apple cannot make this internally. They need a III-V foundry partner.

text:
$SIVE is now aiming to become the next $LITE, a US photonics giant. 

They're re-centering their board around US executives + US photonics.

So the core board are now: US $GFS Executives and $CITI Executives, with the company run by UC Berkeley grads and $LITE executives. 

The 3 members leaving were local Swedish/EU.

This is just a shift in strategy from focusing on developing local Swedish Semi environments:

To dominating the US/global photonics market. I'm not trying to discredit their service/background. 

But in my view to focus around US/global photonics markets, it's likely optimal to have more US executives. 

But they should all be proud for helping make $SIVE what it is today.

text:
Interesting how price action and signals always precede the news.

$POET, $SIVE, $IQE … the photonics story.

Same old speculation pump-and-dump cycle as always.

This time is no different.

The cult of photonics is about to turn on its leaders who told everyone to buy the top.

We warned about this earlier.

Some of these photonics guys are solid fundamental analysts.  We love them. 

Others… who called to buy more at the top…. well.

https://t.co/ZtCLmeqWcm

Sad really.

text:
Opened a starter position in the 'future bottleneck' stock Soitec SA ($SOI.PA), aiming to add on larger dips. 

The stock sits on my Layer 2 (InP & substrates) AI bottleneck chart #InP #Substrates #Photonics $SOI 
 
More: https://t.co/3B6fx7mwms https://t.co/22TjAnEgcw

Photonics value chain in 5 layers. The companies building AI’s optical backbone.

$AXTI – Compound semiconductor substrates. Small, cyclical AI photonics supplier.
$AAOI – Optical transceivers for AI data centers. High risk, high upside.
$LITE – Diversified photonics. Stable, slower growth than AAOI.
$ASML – Only maker of EUV lithography machines. Irreplaceable monopoly.
$ONTO – Semiconductor inspection/metrology tools. Smaller KLAC alternative.

text:
$CRDO into the June 1 print — TD Cowen ($240 PT, Top Pick) frames it well:

The stock is fundamentally misunderstood as a copper-vs-optics binary. It's a vertically integrated connectivity platform across SerDes, AECs, optical DSPs, ZF Optics, and now silicon photonics post-DustPhotonics.

Valuation gap is the setup: ~23x FY27E EPS vs other connectivity names trading north of 45x. That's a 2x multiple gap on a company guiding +75% Y/Y growth in FY27.

What's already in the price (pre-announced intra-quarter):
• DustPhotonics acquisition
• ~$500M FY27 optical revenue target
• +75% Y/Y FY27 growth

What's NOT in the model (pure upside):

• ZF Optics ramping to ~$1B annualized run rate entering FY28
• Active LED Cables — entirely unmodeled
• Cardinal 1.6T DSP design wins taking share from MRVL

FY27 shape: MSD% sequential through 1H, then inflection in 2H driven by optical portfolio ramp. That's the period to own.

TD Cowen's $240 PT is 30x F2028E EPS of $8.00. If the multiple closes toward connectivity peer average, $280-330 is in range over 12-18 months.

Print itself likely muted — bar is set, beats are upside. The grind higher matters more than the gap?

text:
few of these secondary / tertiary layer memory / photonics suppliers have the executive teams and business experience to handle the demand they’re now experiencing at the rate of growth they need to meet.

for the most part they have been leading companies that were in a steady state. 

a rare few have been waiting, perfectly positioned for their moment where the market would see their value. 

peacetime mode was kinda best case.
most were in cockroach mode.
But now, it’s wartime mode.

I expect many of them to fail to grow to meet the challenge ahead of them, even if they have some god given advantage / tech exclusivity.

things I’m looking at—

People: are they hiring / growing headcount aggressively? (are they changing from being who they were to being who they need to be as a company to meet demand)

Cash to growth: capital expenditure on manufacturing assets and ROA (how quickly are they turning new cash into durable growth)

R&D: Expected value of new / previously completed R&D. Is their R&D cycle and approach adjusting to continue to position them ahead of the industry and market’s needs or are they falling behind.

Partnerships: volume / quality / durability / impact of new partnerships launched.

Corporate smell: are the offices and facilities nice and filled with real humans doing real work? 

Bag dumping: are execs restructuring comp structure goals towards wartime growth? Are they offloading too much stock? Are they issuing to many new shares. 

what are you examining besides the tech tree demands and change in stock price?
